title: Chapter 7: The Machine Tightens
content: Day nine brought consequences.

Chloe woke to find her room had been searched. Not obviously — nothing was missing, nothing was broken — but the plant on her desk had been moved two inches from its usual position, and the desk lamp was angled differently, and the journal on her nightstand was open to a page she hadn't left it on.
They were looking for something. They didn't know what. But they knew something was in this room.

She checked the lamp base. The pills were still there — six of them now, two per night, a growing collection of white and pale blue that the institute thought was being absorbed into her bloodstream. They hadn't unscrewed the base. They'd just looked at the lamp and moved on.
Amateurs, Chloe thought again. But the search meant something. Holloway had escalated. The "increased observation" wasn't just a note in a file anymore. It was physical.
At breakfast, Layla sat down across from her with a face that said *we need to talk* before her mouth opened.
"They audited the network this morning," Layla said, voice low. "I saw the logs before they locked me out."
"Locked you out of what?"
"Everything. Internal messaging. The cafeteria terminal. Even the patient Wi-Fi — the one that was supposed to be for 'approved therapeutic content.' It's all dark."

The second floor was restricted for a reason. It wasn't just where administration lived — it was where they kept the patients who'd been there longest. The ones who'd "graduated" from the first-floor program. The ones who were supposed to be success stories.
In reality, Maya had learned, the second floor was where they put the patients who knew too much.

Rachel Okonkwo — no relation, but Maya had felt a kinship the moment she'd heard the name — had been a practicing psychiatrist at UCSF before she published a paper in The Lancet. The paper documented a pattern she'd noticed across her career: wealthy families using private psychiatric facilities to control daughters who embarrassed them. She'd called it "therapeutic containment" — a clinical term for what was essentially legal kidnapping.
Three months after publication, her own family had committed her. For "burnout." For "delusional ideation." For the crime of knowing too much and saying it too loudly.
She'd been inside Serenity for three years.

Maya had been trying to reach her for two days. The problem wasn't access — Maya could get to the second floor during the afternoon "cross-level social hour," the one hour per day when first and second-floor patients were allowed to mingle. The problem was that Rachel didn't mingle. She sat in the corner of the second-floor common room, reading the same copy of The New England Journal of Medicine she'd been reading for three years, and spoke to no one.

"She's broken," Sophia said when Maya asked about her. "Not broken like the rest of us. Broken differently. She came in here thinking she could fix the system from inside. Three years later, she's still inside and the system isn't fixed."
"So she gave up."
"She learned," Sophia said, "that fighting alone doesn't work."
Maya filed that. Fighting alone doesn't work. Rachel hadn't broken — she'd run out of allies.
And Maya knew exactly what to do with someone who'd run out of allies.
She found them more.
Rachel's hand, which had been resting on the journal, went very still.
No one in Serenity used that word. Not out loud. Not even in the laundry room, with the dryers screaming. "Fix" was the approved vocabulary. "Heal." "Progress." "Wellness."
"Burn" was a diagnosis.

"Tell Chloe," Rachel said, her voice different now — lower, faster, the voice of someone who'd been quiet for three years and had forgotten what it felt like to speak at full volume, "that if she's serious, I have something she needs."
"What?"

"The patient files. All of them. Not the medical ones — the real ones. The intake interviews where families explain why they're really committing their daughters." Rachel met Maya's eyes. "I've been memorizing them for three years."
Maya's pulse hit pre-race levels.
"How many?"
"Seventeen current patients. Twenty-three former ones." Rachel's jaw tightened. "I know why every woman in this building is here. And I know which ones will fight if someone gives them a reason."
Maya stood up. "Cross-level social hour ends in ten minutes. I'll be back tomorrow."
"Don't come alone," Rachel said. "Bring Chloe. I want to see if she's real."
"She's real."
"Everyone thinks their leader is real." Rachel picked up her journal again. "Most of them aren't."
